questions.question_text: Which properties require a Property Condition Disclosure Statement under Mississippi law?
questions.explanation: Mississippi's Property Condition Disclosure law (§§89-1-501 through 89-1-527) requires a PCDS for transfers of residential property of one to four dwelling units when the transfer is made by, or with the aid of, a licensed real estate broker or salesperson.

questions.question_text: A seller is unsure whether the roof has ever leaked but has never seen evidence of a leak. What standard applies to completing the PCDS?
questions.explanation: The seller completes the PCDS based on actual knowledge using a good faith standard. The seller is not required to conduct inspections or hire professionals to discover unknown conditions.

questions.question_text: When must the Property Condition Disclosure Statement be delivered to the buyer?
questions.correct_answer: As soon as practicable before closing
distractors: Within 10 days of the listing agreement
Within 48 hours of the accepted offer
questions.explanation: Mississippi law requires the PCDS to be delivered as soon as practicable before the transfer of title. It should be provided early in the transaction to give the buyer time to review.

questions.question_text: Is the PCDS considered part of the purchase contract in Mississippi?
questions.correct_answer: No, it is a standalone disclosure document
distractors: Yes, it is a contract addendum
Yes, it must be incorporated by reference
No, and it is filed with MREC separately
questions.explanation: The PCDS is not part of the purchase contract — MREC guidance states it should not be included in or become part of any contract between seller and buyer. The seller typically completes it when the property is listed, but it is a standalone disclosure, not a contract term.

questions.question_text: Must a Mississippi seller hire a home inspector before completing the PCDS?
distractors: Yes, a licensed inspector is mandatory
Only for properties over 20 years old
Only if the property has a septic system
questions.explanation: Mississippi law does not require the seller to conduct any inspections before completing the PCDS. The seller discloses based only on actual knowledge of the property's condition.

questions.question_text: A buyer asks if a previous owner died in the home. Must the seller disclose this on the PCDS?
questions.correct_answer: No, death on property is exempt
distractors: Yes, all material facts must be disclosed
Only if the death was violent
Only if it occurred within 3 years
questions.explanation: Death on the property (natural death, suicide, or homicide) is a nonmaterial fact under §89-1-527 that need not be disclosed, and failure to disclose it creates no cause of action. However, the seller may not intentionally misrepresent the facts in response to a direct question — lying to the buyer remains actionable under §89-1-527(3).

questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT exempt from the PCDS requirement in Mississippi?
questions.explanation: New construction is NOT exempt from the PCDS requirement in Mississippi. The builder/seller must still complete the disclosure form. Court-ordered sales, foreclosures, and stigmatizing factors are among the standard exemptions.

questions.question_text: A previous occupant had a communicable disease. Must this be disclosed on the Mississippi PCDS?
questions.correct_answer: No, prior occupant disease is exempt
distractors: Only if it was a reportable disease
Only if the buyer specifically asks
questions.explanation: Under §89-1-527, the fact that an occupant had a disease not known to be transmitted through common occupancy of real estate (including HIV and AIDS) is not a material fact and need not be disclosed; failure to disclose it creates no cause of action.

questions.question_text: A felony was committed in a home being sold. Is this a required disclosure item in Mississippi?
questions.correct_answer: No, felonies on property are exempt
distractors: Yes, all criminal activity must be disclosed
questions.explanation: A felony committed on the property is a nonmaterial fact under §89-1-527 that need not be disclosed — with one exception: illegal drug activity that affects the physical condition of the property (such as meth contamination) is not shielded and must be disclosed.

questions.question_text: A foreclosed property is being sold by the lender. Is a PCDS required?
distractors: Yes, all residential sales require it
Only if the property has known defects
Only if the lender occupied the home
questions.explanation: Foreclosure sales are among the standard exemptions from Mississippi's PCDS requirements. The lender typically lacks actual knowledge of the property's condition.

questions.question_text: A listing agent notices the seller left several PCDS sections blank. What should the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Advise the seller to complete it
distractors: Fill in the blanks for the seller
File the incomplete form with MREC
questions.explanation: Advising the seller to complete the disclosure remains the appropriate practice, and the agent should never fill in the form — MREC's instructions direct the seller to complete it personally. Note that since 2023, licensees have no statutory duty regarding the PCDS and cannot be disciplined by MREC over it (§§89-1-503(2), 89-1-505(5), 89-1-523(2)); an incomplete PCDS puts the buyer on notice to inquire of the seller.

questions.question_text: After the PCDS is delivered, the seller discovers a major foundation crack. What happens next?
questions.correct_answer: The seller must deliver an amended PCDS
distractors: The seller must repair the crack first
Nothing unless the buyer orders an inspection
questions.explanation: A seller who acquires knowledge that makes the original PCDS materially inaccurate must deliver an amended PCDS to the buyer as soon as practicable. Delivery of a material amendment restarts the buyer's right to terminate — 3 days after in-person delivery or 5 days after deposit in the mail (§§89-1-503, 89-1-515).

questions.question_text: A seller mails the PCDS to the buyer after the contract is signed. How many days does the buyer have to terminate?
questions.explanation: When the PCDS is delivered after an offer is executed, the buyer may terminate by written notice within 5 days after the PCDS is deposited in the mail, or within 3 days after in-person delivery (§89-1-503).

questions.question_text: A buyer receives a late PCDS hand-delivered at the broker's office. How many days does the buyer have to terminate?
questions.explanation: When the PCDS is delivered late and in person, the buyer has 3 days to terminate the contract. If delivered by mail, the period extends to 5 days.

questions.question_text: A seller willfully omits known termite damage from the PCDS. What exposure does the seller face under current Mississippi law?
distractors: Statutory liability for actual damages under §89-1-523
questions.explanation: A knowingly false or incomplete PCDS can subject the seller to civil claims by the buyer before or after transfer of title — common-law fraud and misrepresentation duties are expressly preserved by §89-1-513. The former §89-1-523 provision imposing statutory liability for actual damages for willful or negligent violations was repealed in 2023 (SB 2647), and noncompliance does not invalidate the transfer.
